docs.title: Building Pharmaceutical Decision Systems
docs.text: A hands-on book that teaches decision science through a complete fictional pharmaceutical launch.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This project explores how pharmaceutical decision science connects evidence to action across a complex, multi-stakeholder market. Through the fictional launch of Roventra, you will learn essential methods for market sizing, patient journeys, competitive intelligence, targeting, measurement, and resource allocation.

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text:
• Chapter 1: A Medicine, a Market, and the Decisions Between Them - Introduction to the Roventra market, key entities, and scope
  • Chapter 2: The Commercialization Operating System - Patient path, launch organization, and operational structure
  • Appendix 2A: Role Definitions

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text:
• Foundational analytics: Market sizing, patient journeys, competitive dynamics, targeting, omnichannel measurement, and resource allocation
  • Cutting-edge methods: Real-world evidence, causal inference, machine learning for treatment-effect estimation, and language models for decision support
  • Hands-on implementation: Working code examples and practical decision science applications

Visit the repository: Building Pharma Decision Systems

docs.title: Chapter 1: A Medicine, a Market, and the Decisions Between Them
docs.text: Welcome to Chapter 1! This chapter introduces the fictional pharmaceutical launch scenario that forms the foundation for the entire book.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: In this chapter, you'll be introduced to: - Roventra: a newly approved medicine entering a competitive market - Key market players: patients, healthcare providers (HCPs), payers, and specialty pharmacies - Core decision challenges: patient reachability, account prioritization, and outcome measurement - The role of decision science: bridging data and action in pharmaceutical launches

docs.title: Read the Full Introduction
docs.text:
👉 Start reading Chapter 1 Introduction

This hands-on guide teaches decision science through a complete fictional pharmaceutical launch, from data infrastructure through to decision-making.

docs.title: Chapter 1: A Medicine, a Market, and the Decisions Between Them
docs.text:
A recently approved medicine enters the market.

Meet the fictional world: Roventra is a once-daily oral medicine for a chronic condition managed mainly by specialists. Two fictional competitors are Nexoral (an established oral medicine) and Vexpro (an established weekly injectable). Real pharmaceutical markets have systematic barriers: missed diagnoses, payer restrictions, pharmacy claim process delays. So does this fictional world.

The FDA approval signals the start. After approval, the team must answer three core questions: patient reachability, account prioritization, and outcome causation. The forecast team has predicted the commercial launch trajectory, but outcomes still depend on diagnosis, clinical prescribing, payer coverage, pharmacy fulfillment, and field team effort. These decisions often happen independently, with incomplete information about the others. Decision science is a discipline that addresses this gap: between having data and knowing what to change.

This is a hands-on book that teaches decision science through a complete fictional pharmaceutical launch. You will build synthetic data infrastructure, write queries against databases, perform data cleaning, stitch data sources together, construct data science and machine learning models, analyze patient journeys and competitive dynamics, and design measurements to test whether your team's efforts worked. You'll see why some decisions fail, when data clarifies the issue, and how to act with incomplete or partial evidence. By the end, you will understand how pharmaceutical decision science connects evidence to action across a complex, multi-stakeholder market.

docs.title: The Roventra World
docs.text:
These entities appear throughout the book and form the market structure.

  • Patient PAT02034: a patient with the condition, eligible for Roventra treatment.
  • HCP HCP0280: the clinician who writes the prescription, later becomes a targeting priority and measurement unit.
  • Account ACC089: the clinic where HCP0280 practices, the place where access friction, field prioritization, and call planning happen.
  • Payer PAY002: the organization whose formulary decisions determine patient access to medication.
  • Roventra 90000-1001-11, Nexoral 90000-1002-11, Vexpro 90000-1003-11: three medicines competing for clinical choice, formulary position, and treatment share.

Figure 1.1. Roventra enters a market shaped by the patient, clinical choice, payer access, and specialty-pharmacy fulfillment. Fictional market.

docs.title: Scope: Foundations and Cutting-Edge Methods
docs.text:
This book discusses the essential decision science methods that pharmaceutical teams use daily, but are often isolated in academic research, proprietary systems, or institutional knowledge. The early chapters focus on foundational analytics: market sizing, patient journeys, competitive intelligence, HCP and account targeting, omnichannel measurement, and resource allocation. These methods are not novel, but they form the practical backbone of commercial decision-making.

The book also incorporates cutting-edge techniques alongside foundations. You will find real-world evidence and causal inference methods, machine learning for treatment-effect estimation, and fine-tuned large (or small) language models (LLMs, SLMs) for decision support. Some advanced content appears in companion notebooks and appendices due to space constraints, but all methods are demonstrated with working code. The goal is to equip analysts and leaders with both the foundational tools and the cutting-edge techniques that move real decisions.

docs.title: Figure 1.1 Source Prompt
docs.text: Generated with the built-in OpenAI image generation tool on June 12, 2026.

docs.title: Teaching Objective
docs.text: Show the fictional market surrounding a newly approved medicine. The medicine sits at the center, while patients, clinical choice, access, fulfillment, customer engagement, and evidence determine whether it reaches treatment and creates commercial value.

docs.title: Final Revision Prompt
docs.text:
Edit the existing Chapter 1 illustration. Preserve its elegant flat editorial vector style,
white background, restrained pastel blue, muted gold, soft green, and small coral accents.
Simplify the composition drastically.

Make the fictional medicine the unmistakable center of a small external market. Keep one
simple unbranded medicine carton and pill bottle on a clean circular platform in the center.
Around it, show only 4 essential external actors as simplified half-body editorial figures or
compact vignettes, evenly spaced with generous whitespace: 1 patient, 1 specialist HCP, 1
payer reviewer, and 1 specialty-pharmacy professional. Give each actor only 1 small
identifying object. Do not include internal company teams.

Remove all unnecessary environmental detail: no clinic room, shelves, buildings, plants,
furniture, background monitors, geographic map, road network, crowd silhouettes, decorative
architecture, or regulatory building.

Use a wide, balanced composition. The medicine platform occupies the center. The actors form
a loose, open circle around it, clearly belonging to one fictional market. A few subtle curved
ground lines may unify them. No arrows, flowchart, boxes, or concentric infographic rings.

The image should communicate that one newly approved medicine creates a market made of
patients, clinical choice, access, fulfillment, customer engagement, and evidence.

Keep the mood calm, prepared, practical, and consequential. Use no labels, logos, brand names,
or readable interface text. Avoid childish cartoon art, corporate stock art, futuristic
visuals, glowing effects, photorealism, marketing hype, duplicated people, and watermark.

docs.title: Label Revision Prompt
docs.text:
Preserve the existing illustration exactly: same wide composition, 4 people, faces, poses,
medicine package, circular platform, white background, connecting lines, restrained pastel
palette, clean editorial vector style, and generous whitespace.

Add only restrained typographic labels in a clean dark-gray sans-serif font:

- On the medicine carton: "Roventra"
- Near the patient: "Patient · PAT02034"
- Near the doctor: "HCP · HCP0280"
- Near the payer reviewer: "Payer · PAY002"
- Near the fulfillment professional: "Specialty pharmacy"

Keep the labels visually secondary. Do not add badges, boxes, arrows, a title, subtitle,
legend, NDC number, "virtual world" wording, logos, extra interface text, or watermark.

docs.title: Internal-Team Removal
docs.text: The final revision removes the field representative and decision-science analyst from Figure 1.1. They are internal commercial functions and now appear in Figure 2.3, where the book introduces the organization supporting the patient path.

docs.title: Composition Revision
docs.text: The specialty-pharmacy professional sits in the lower-left opening and faces right toward the medicine. The payer moved to the lower-right corner, opposite specialty pharmacy. This balances the patient and HCP above the platform while keeping all 4 external actors oriented toward the fictional market.

docs.title: Chapter 2: The Commercialization Operating System
docs.text: Welcome to Chapter 2! This chapter explores the organizational structure and decision points that drive a pharmaceutical launch.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: In this chapter, you'll discover: - The patient path: the journey from clinical prescription to treatment initiation - Five critical checkpoints: where different parties (HCP, account, payer, specialty pharmacy, patient) control the path forward - The launch organization: which teams support each stage of the patient journey

docs.title: Read the Full Chapter
docs.text:
👉 Start reading Chapter 2: The Commercialization Operating System

Also available: - 📋 Appendix 2A: Detailed Role Definitions

This chapter maps the real operational structure behind successful pharmaceutical launches.

docs.title: Appendix 2A: Launch Team Role Reference
docs.text: This appendix defines the functions named in Chapter 2. Reporting lines and titles vary by company. The table groups roles by the work they support in the fictional Roventra launch.

docs.title: Field and Customer Support
docs.text: Role Primary responsibility Boundary Sales Representative Provides approved product information to HCPs and records customer interactions Clinical and coverage decisions remain with HCPs and payers District or Regional Sales Manager Sets field priorities, coaches representatives, and manages execution Works within the approved strategy and promotional rules Key Account Manager Coordinates work with health systems, integrated delivery networks, and large accounts Account committees retain their clinical protocol and purchasing decisions Field Reimbursement Manager Helps offices understand payer processes, prior authorization, and appeals The account completes clinical records and the payer determines coverage Medical Science Liaison Conducts scientific exchange and responds to unsolicited clinical questions Sits in Medical Affairs and remains separate from promotional direction Nurse Educator Provides approved education on treatment use and patient support The treating clinician retains clinical responsibility

docs.title: Marketing
docs.text: Function Primary responsibility Typical output Brand and HCP Marketing Develops positioning, messages, and HCP-facing programs Brand plan, campaign, approved field material Patient Marketing Provides patient-facing education where regulations allow Patient campaign, website, educational material Disease-State Education Builds unbranded awareness of the condition and its burden Disease education program Omnichannel and Digital Marketing Coordinates email, web, webinar, and other channel activity Channel plan and contact sequence Marketing Operations Manages content production and review workflow Approved content and production schedule

docs.title: Market Access and Reimbursement
docs.text: Function Primary responsibility Typical output Payer Account Director Leads account engagement with health plans and pharmacy-benefit managers Account plan and formulary discussion Pricing and Contracting Develops price, rebate, and contract terms Contract proposal and financial assessment Trade and Distribution Manages product movement through distributors and pharmacies Distribution agreement and channel plan Health Economics and Outcomes Research Develops economic and outcomes evidence for healthcare decision-makers Value evidence and economic analysis Policy and Government Affairs Tracks policy, legislation, and public coverage developments Policy assessment and response plan

docs.title: Patient Services
docs.text: Function Primary responsibility Boundary Benefits Investigation Confirms reported coverage requirements and patient cost information Provides current information without deciding coverage Prior-Authorization Support Helps offices identify forms and route required documentation The account supplies clinical records and the payer evaluates them Manufacturer Affordability Program Provides support to eligible patients under program rules Eligibility and use must follow legal and program requirements Independent Charitable Foundation Makes assistance decisions under its own governance Operates independently from the manufacturer Adherence and Persistence Support Provides approved reminders, education, and navigation The patient and treating clinician retain treatment decisions Specialty-Pharmacy Coordination Tracks referral, authorization, dispensing, and delivery steps The pharmacy applies its dispensing and operational requirements

docs.title: Operations, Analytics, and Leadership
docs.text: Function Primary responsibility Typical output Sales Operations Manages incentive compensation, territory alignment, and call planning Alignment, target list, call plan Commercial Operations Coordinates processes, platforms, and execution across the commercial organization Operating plan and performance process Data, CRM, and Commercial Technology Maintains customer data, interaction systems, and analytical platforms Governed data products and system records Decision Science and Analytics Turns data into evidence for choices about patients, accounts, channels, and resources Analysis, recommendation, measurement plan Forecasting Projects demand under explicit assumptions and scenarios Forecast and scenario range Market Research Studies customer attitudes, needs, and decision drivers Research report and insight Competitive Intelligence Tracks competitor, market, and access changes Competitive assessment Brand Lead Directs the brand strategy and plan Brand priorities and cross-functional decisions Commercial Lead or General Manager Holds responsibility for commercial performance and resource choices Business direction and resource allocation Launch Lead Coordinates readiness and execution across functions Integrated launch plan and readiness review

docs.title: Cross-Functional Partners
docs.text: Partner Primary responsibility Boundary Regulatory Reviews promotional claims and materials against the approved label and applicable requirements Commercial leadership sets business targets Legal and Compliance Interprets legal requirements and establishes compliant processes Business owners remain accountable for their decisions Finance Funds the plan and evaluates financial performance Clinical and coverage policy remain with the responsible external parties Supply Chain Plans inventory and product availability through the distribution network Payers retain approval decisions and pharmacies retain dispensing decisions Medical Affairs Leads scientific exchange and evidence work outside promotional direction Maintains separation from Commercial promotion

docs.title: Chapter 2: The Commercialization Operating System
docs.text: FDA approval cleared Roventra for sale. The launch team now has to turn the approved label, payer plans, field options, supply plans, and patient-support programs into treatment for real patients. The first question is what the final label changed and where the existing launch plan must adjust.

docs.title: 2.1 Decision Points Along the Patient Path
docs.text:
Between a doctor's prescription and a patient's first dose sit several decisions and handoffs. A physician judges that the patient fits Roventra's approved indication and writes the order. From there, the prescription moves through checkpoints controlled by different parties: the account manages documentation, the payer evaluates coverage, the specialty pharmacy arranges fulfillment, and the patient decides whether to start therapy. The cross-functional launch team cannot control these external decisions, but it can remove friction, provide timely information, and measure where patients stop.

Figure 2.1. The path along the patient prescription to treatment. Fictional market.

Table 2.1 lists the five checkpoints and what prevents a prescription from advancing at each one.

Table 2.1. The external decision-makers on the patient path.

Party What they control What can delay treatment HCP Clinical fit and treatment selection The patient falls outside the approved indication, or the HCP selects Nexoral or Vexpro Account Documentation and workflow Missing chart notes, insurance information, test results, or required forms Payer Coverage eligibility and conditions Formulary restriction, prior authorization, step therapy, or new-to-market review Specialty pharmacy Coordination and fulfillment Benefit confirmation, affordability, dispensing, or delivery Patient Treatment initiation and persistence Affordability, concerns about treatment, side effects, or a decision not to start or continue

The launch team supports each of these checkpoints. Section 2.2 shows which organizational functions support each stage.

docs.title: 2.2 The Launch Organization Behind the Path
docs.text:
Roventra's launch team brings together Commercial and partners from Medical Affairs, Legal and Compliance, Regulatory, Finance, and Supply Chain. Launch leadership (the brand lead, commercial lead, and launch lead) connects the work across these groups through brand strategy, commercial accountability, and cross-functional readiness. Table 2.2 maps those groups to the path. Detailed role definitions are in Appendix 2A.

Table 2.2. The launch groups that support each stage of the patient path.

Path stage External decision owner Launch groups that support the stage Support the team can provide Diagnosis and disease awareness HCP and patient Disease-state marketing (unbranded), Medical Affairs, Market Research Disease education and evidence about unmet need Treatment selection HCP Field Sales, Brand and Omnichannel Marketing, Medical Affairs Approved product education and scientific exchange through the appropriate channel Prescription and documentation Account Field Reimbursement, Key Account Management, Patient Services Current process guidance and help routing required documentation Coverage Payer Market Access, Pricing and Contracting, HEOR, Policy Formulary discussions, contract terms, and value evidence Fulfillment Specialty pharmacy Trade and Distribution, Patient Services, Supply Chain Benefit coordination, product availability, dispensing support, and delivery coordination Initiation and persistence Patient Patient Services, affordability programs, Nurse Educators, patient marketing Navigation, eligible affordability support, approved education, and adherence support All stages Launch leadership Commercial and Sales Operations, Decision Science and Analytics, Finance, Legal and Compliance, Regulatory Priorities, resources, measurement, approved processes, and governance

Figure 2.2. Launch functions and cross-functional partners support different points along the patient path. Fictional market.

docs.title: 2.3 Into the Data
docs.text:
The commercialization operating system maps the patient path to the organizational teams that support it.

The patient path creates partial records in isolated systems. Chapter 3 assembles these separate records into a synthetic data layer and tests what each source can legitimately support for decision-making.

docs.title: Chapter 3: A Synthetic Lab for Real Pharma Questions
docs.text: Welcome to Chapter 3! This chapter teaches you to work with pharmaceutical data by building and analyzing a transparent synthetic dataset.

docs.title: What You'll Learn
docs.text: In this chapter, you'll discover: - The pharmaceutical data landscape: Claims, EHR, formulary, CRM, and public data sources - Data maturity and receipt lags: Why recent claims are incomplete and how to handle them - Patient journeys across systems: Following one patient from clinical order through pharmacy fill - Data quality auditing: Identifying what each table really counts and what it misses - Synthetic data generation: Building realistic pharmaceutical datasets with known structure and defects

docs.text:
